claim: Skills (folders with markdown, scripts, and assets) are minimal form factor for packaging procedural knowledge; simpler than traditional tools, more versionable than prompts
actionable: Replace tool calls with script-based skills in agent workflows; organize domain knowledge as composable skill folders; version skills in Git
source_title: Don't Build Agents, Build Skills Instead – Barry Zhang & Mahesh Murag, Anthropic

claim: Traditional tools have ambiguous instructions; agents can't modify broken tools mid-execution. Scripts as tools are self-documenting and modifiable by agents
actionable: Wrap Python/bash scripts as skills instead of abstract tool definitions; let agents debug and improve scripts via code modification
source_title: Don't Build Agents, Build Skills Instead – Barry Zhang & Mahesh Murag, Anthropic

claim: Skills are progressively disclosed: only metadata shown initially; full skill.md loaded at runtime when needed, protecting context window for hundreds of skills
actionable: Use skill metadata for discovery; lazy-load full skill content only when agent decides to use it; enables massive skill libraries without context bloat
source_title: Don't Build Agents, Build Skills Instead – Barry Zhang & Mahesh Murag, Anthropic

claim: Skills enable three ecosystem layers: foundational (general capabilities), third-party (partner integrations), enterprise (org-specific best practices)
actionable: Build foundational skills for your domain; integrate third-party skills from ecosystem partners; create enterprise skills encoding organizational practices
source_title: Don't Build Agents, Build Skills Instead – Barry Zhang & Mahesh Murag, Anthropic

claim: Enterprise use: large teams using skills to teach agents about code style, organizational best practices, bespoke internal software without rebuilding agents
actionable: Use skills to distribute organizational knowledge to agent deployments; encode team practices once, apply across all agent instances
source_title: Don't Build Agents, Build Skills Instead – Barry Zhang & Mahesh Murag, Anthropic

claim: Non-technical users (finance, legal, recruiting) are building skills; democratizes agent customization without requiring coding expertise
actionable: Simplify skill creation UX; enable domain experts to build skills without writing code; provide skill templates for common use cases
source_title: Don't Build Agents, Build Skills Instead – Barry Zhang & Mahesh Murag, Anthropic

claim: Skills + MCP servers pattern: MCP provides external connectivity, skills provide domain expertise; agents equipped with both achieve specialized verticalization
actionable: Combine MCP servers for connectivity with domain-specific skills; co-deploy both to enable agents for specialized professional work
source_title: Don't Build Agents, Build Skills Instead – Barry Zhang & Mahesh Murag, Anthropic

claim: Agents should write their own skills from experience; standardized skill format enables continuous learning and skill creation by agents
actionable: Design agent workflows to enable skill creation; let agents capture discovered patterns as new skills; implement agent skill self-improvement loops
source_title: Don't Build Agents, Build Skills Instead – Barry Zhang & Mahesh Murag, Anthropic

claim: Model is processor, runtime is OS, skills are applications; skills open application layer for developers to encode domain expertise and unique approaches
actionable: Think of agent runtime as platform; build skills as application layer where domain-specific value is captured
source_title: Don't Build Agents, Build Skills Instead – Barry Zhang & Mahesh Murag, Anthropic

claim: Future roadmap: test/eval tooling for skills, versioning lineage tracking, explicit skill dependencies, cross-skill composition, and community skill distribution
actionable: Plan for skill versioning and testing infrastructure; implement dependency management for skill composition; build skill marketplace/distribution
source_title: Don't Build Agents, Build Skills Instead – Barry Zhang & Mahesh Murag, Anthropic
